coredata.dc:title: “Five birds one stone” tri-modal monitoring driven lab-on-magnetic aptasensor for accurate pathogen detection and enhanced germicidal application
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Yuwei
Cao, Lulu
Jiao, Rui
Zhang, Xiyan
Zhao, Hong yuan
Liang, Zhao
Li, Guoqing
Ling, Na
Ye, Yingwang
coredata.dc:description: The effective combination of ultra-precise detection and on-demand sterilization stands out as one of the most valuable antifouling methods to combat pathogenic bacteria source and ensure the environment and food safety. Herein, an innovative “five birds one stone” aptasensor has been reported. It integrates magnetic separation, tri-modal precision detection, and efficient sterilization for monitoring of Staphylococcus aureus. Firstly, as a switch of the aptasensor, aptamer-modified potassium chloride-doped carbon dots (apt/KCl@CDs) could be adsorbed onto the surface of magnetic multi-walled carbon nanotube composites (M-MWCNTs) through π-π stacking, which could be replaced by the specific binding of the target bacteria to the aptamer. The mutual interference between the nanomaterials could be eliminated by this reverse magnetosorption strategy, enhancing the test sensitivity. In addition to the fluorescence properties, the peroxidase activity possessed by apt/KCl@CDs enables the conversion of the (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine) TMB-H2O2 colorimetric system to a photothermal modal. Then, the ultra-precision detection in the assay was achieved by the fluorescence-colorimetric-photothermal tri-modal sensing from the formation of S. aureus-apt/KCl@CDs in the supernatant. Besides, the efficient sterilization could be ensured by adsorbing the apt/KCl@CDs on the surface of S. aureus, generating toxic •OH for direct attacking cells. This was the first report that was more beneficial for bacterial eradication. The detection limits of fluorescence, colorimetric and photothermal modals were 4.81 cfu/mL, 3.40 cfu/mL and 6.74 cfu/mL, respectively. The magnetic nanoplatform integrating tri-modal detection-sterilization meets the demand for highly sensitive and precise detection in different scenarios, providing immediate control for pathogens and broad application prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Potassium chloride-doped carbon dots
Tri-modal detection
Staphylococcus aureus
Enhanced sterilization
Monitoring integration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Exploration of the optimization of conditions
Detection of Staphylococcus aureus in pure culture by aptasensor
Practicality of tri-modal detection in real samples
Integrated platform for the elimination of Staphylococcus aureus

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development program , China ( 2022YFF1100702 ; 2022YFF1100703 ); the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32230084 ; 32102104 ); the Guangdong Provincial Key Research and Development Project (2022B1111040002); and the Anhui Natural Science Foundation (2208085J11).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of “five birds one stone” magnetic nanoplatform.

Characterization and properties of apt/KCl@CDs. (A) TEM image of KCl@CDs. Scale bar: 50 nm, EDS elemental (C, O, K, Cl) mapping images (B)–(F) of KCl@CDs. (G) EPR spectrum of DMPO following incubation with KCl@CDs (4 mg/mL) + H2O2 (1.0 mM). (H) Fluorescence spectra of apt/KCl@CDs and KCl@CDs. (I) Absorption spectra of aptamer (1), apt/KCl@CDs (2) and KCl@CDs (3). (J) DLS analysis of apt/KCl@CDs and KCl@CDs. (K) Zeta potentials of apt/KCl@CDs and KCl@CDs.

Exploration of the optimization of conditions. Effect of temperature (A), incubation time (B), pH (C) and concentration of apt/KCl@CDs (D) in the colorimetric mode detection. Effect of the incubation time (F), concentration of apt/KCl@CDs (G), pH (H) in the fluorescence mode detection. Effect of laser irradiation time (J), concentration of apt/KCl@CDs (K). (E), (I), (L) represents tri-modal monitoring driven lab-on-magnetic aptasensor under different conditions, (I): M-MWCNTs-apt/KCl@CDs + 106 cfu/mL of S. aureus, (II): M-MWCNTs-KCl@CDs + 0 cfu/mL of S. aureus, (III): M-MWCNTs + 106 cfu/mL of S. aureus, (IV) Buffer + 106 cfu/mL of S. aureus.

Detection of S. aureus by the tri-modal aptasensor under pure culture. (A) Detection principle of tri-modal sensing platform. (B)–(D) Detection performance of fluorescence modal for 0-108 cfu/mL S. aureus. (E)–(G) Detection performance of colorimetric modal for 0-108 cfu/mL S. aureus. (H)–(J) Detection performance of photothermal modal for 0-108 cfu/mL S. aureus. (K)–(M) Analysis of aptasensor specificity driven by tri-modal detection.

Integrated platform for the elimination of S. aureus. (A) Presentation of S. aureus through plate counting, SEM and fluorescence microscope under different conditions. (B) Analysis of survival rate of S. aureus under different conditions. (C) Release amount of LDH. (D) Concentration of PO4–P. (E) Oxidation of TMB when H2O2 is added to different materials. 1: KCl@CDs, 2: CDs, 3: Control. (F) Effect of the presence or absence of aptamer modification on the sterilization efficiency of KCl@CDs. (G) Integration platform for sterilization applications in real samples. (H) Principle of enhancing bactericidal efficiency with aptamer.
Application of tri-modal aptasensor in actual samples.

head.ce:title: “Five birds one stone” tri-modal monitoring driven lab-on-magnetic aptasensor for accurate pathogen detection and enhanced germicidal application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei, 230009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Horticulture Research, Anhui Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Hefei, 230031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Horticulture Research, Anhui Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Hefei, 230031, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Tunxi Road 193, Hefei, Anhui province, 230009, China
Corresponding author. Tunxi Road 193, Hefei, Anhui province, 230009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effective combination of ultra-precise detection and on-demand sterilization stands out as one of the most valuable antifouling methods to combat pathogenic bacteria source and ensure the environment and food safety. Herein, an innovative “five birds one stone” aptasensor has been reported. It integrates magnetic separation, tri-modal precision detection, and efficient sterilization for monitoring of Staphylococcus aureus. Firstly, as a switch of the aptasensor, aptamer-modified potassium chloride-doped carbon dots (apt/KCl@CDs) could be adsorbed onto the surface of magnetic multi-walled carbon nanotube composites (M-MWCNTs) through π-π stacking, which could be replaced by the specific binding of the target bacteria to the aptamer. The mutual interference between the nanomaterials could be eliminated by this reverse magnetosorption strategy, enhancing the test sensitivity. In addition to the fluorescence properties, the peroxidase activity possessed by apt/KCl@CDs enables the conversion of the (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine) TMB-H2O2 colorimetric system to a photothermal modal. Then, the ultra-precision detection in the assay was achieved by the fluorescence-colorimetric-photothermal tri-modal sensing from the formation of S. aureus-apt/KCl@CDs in the supernatant. Besides, the efficient sterilization could be ensured by adsorbing the apt/KCl@CDs on the surface of S. aureus, generating toxic •OH for direct attacking cells. This was the first report that was more beneficial for bacterial eradication. The detection limits of fluorescence, colorimetric and photothermal modals were 4.81 cfu/mL, 3.40 cfu/mL and 6.74 cfu/mL, respectively. The magnetic nanoplatform integrating tri-modal detection-sterilization meets the demand for highly sensitive and precise detection in different scenarios, providing immediate control for pathogens and broad application prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most widespread foodborne pathogens, Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) has been reported in multiple environmental and food samples, such as water, air, and animal excreta (C. Wang et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2022). The serious human diseases can be caused by S. aureus with low-dose infection, including sepsis, pneumonia, enteritis, pericarditis, and even systemic infections (H. Wang et al., 2021). However, the traditional culture method and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) for the diagnosis of S. aureus are labor-intensive, require highly technical personnel, and can result in a high false-negative rate (Li et al., 2023). In addition, although sterilization techniques such as UV light and bleach can effectively inactivate microorganisms, their biocompatibility is poor and harmful to humans. Overuse of antibiotics can make humans susceptible to the effects of multidrug-resistant bacteria, biofilms, and intracellular infections.

Colorimetric and fluorescence-based biosensors have been developed for the rapid detection of S. aureus to solve these limitations. In the research of Pan et al. (2022), specific peptides were coupled to PEGylated magnetic nanoclusters and then combined with fluorescent probes. They succeeded in achieving specific capture and highly sensitive detection of S. aureus from serum. On the basis of the oligonucleotide-mediated oxidase mimetic activity of octahedral Mn3O4, Zhu et al. (2021) also established a novel label-free, ultrasensitive colorimetric aptasensor for the detection of S. aureus in food. Nevertheless, the majority of these biosensors use a single signal strategy in the samples, which is disturbed by the sample media and results in false positive signal output. Furthermore, it also has been developed of fluorescence-colorimetric dual-modal probes (Ouyang et al., 2021) and dual-colorimetric detection methods (Liu et al., 2023). Unfortunately, it is an important factor affecting the signal output that the different functional nanoparticles interfere with each other. Currently, this interference is mainly gotten rid of by magnetic separation, which is mainly driven by magnetic beads (Luo et al., 2020). However, the loading of detection material is limited by the restricted specific surface area of magnetic beads, and the functionalization of magnetic beads also increases costs. Therefore, it is crucial that develop a more precise and cheap detection of S. aureus. Worse than that, most nanomaterial-based strategies are based on the presence of separate sensing and disinfection forms of bacteria, and often with no effective countermeasures following a positive sensor test. The above challenges are simplified with the discovery of multifunctional nanozymes. They frequently exhibit a variety of properties, for example, fluorescence, enzymatic activity, and photothermal. (Xu et al., 2023a; Xu et al., 2023b). The rapid development of low-cost carbon-based nanozymes has been recently advocated by many researchers (Gao et al., 2023), but they are often generated by doping with toxic organic substances and heavy metals, the preparation process is also cumbersome and unfriendly to environment. Obviously, it is crucial that develop and applicate of non-toxic carbon-based nanozymes. Based on the above, there is an urgent need to develop a “multi birds one stone” integrated platform to achieve accurate multimodal detection of S. aureus, while ensuring the reduction of inter-nanomaterial interference effects to improve its sensitivity. Furthermore, after a positive result, the target bacteria can be eliminated in time, then it will become a convenient, low-cost, and rapid novel strategy.

Herein, we used lignin, the second most renewable and sustainable resource in nature, as the precursor to synthesize the KCl@CDs nanozyme. Specific recognition of S. aureus is achieved using aptamers. Aptamers are small oligonucleotide sequences obtained through in vitro screening that play a critical role in improving the specificity and sensitivity of the sensor. In the presence of the target, the aptamer undergoes a 3D conformational change to create a specific binding site for the target and achieve specific binding to the target (Song et al., 2023). After functionalization of KCl@CDs with aptamers (apt/KCl@CDs), apt/KCl@CDs acquired the recognition-colorimetric-fluorescence triple property (Fig. 1 A). Simultaneously, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), which adsorb nucleotide single chains via π-π stacking, exhibited good adsorption carrier action and magnetic separation after successful chemical coupling with carbonyl iron powder (CIP) (Fig. 1B). As shown in Fig. 1C, when S. aureus was present in an environmental sample, apt/KCl@CDs adsorbed on M-MWCNTs could specifically capture the target bacteria, resulting in S. aureus-apt/KCl@CDs being detached on M-MWCNTs. The interference of the carrier on the display signal was removed by magnetic adsorption of M-MWCNTs and the supernatant was taken. On the one hand, the fluorescence signal intensity in the supernatant was linearly related to the concentration of the target bacteria, and on the other hand, the colorimetric monitoring was implemented by using the peroxidase activity of KCl@CDs, which catalyzes the bluing of TMB. The blue product was irradiated with a 660 nm laser and the temperature signal was found to depend on the concentration of bacteria, thus permitting photothermal detection. Thus, the implementation of tri-modal detection with built-in cross-reference correction was completed. After determining that the test results were positive, the extermination of S. aureus was implemented. Shedding of apt/KCl@CDs on M-MWCNTs specifically captures target bacteria to form S. aureus-apt/KCl@CDs complex. Due to the peroxidase activity of KCl@CDs, it could catalyze the •OH produced by H2O2 to cover the bacteria for efficient sterilization. As a novel perspective, ultra-precise detection and on-demand sterilization can be flexibly applied according to the actual situation, saving unnecessary energy consumption and reducing costs. In addition, it avoids the problem of secondary contamination of the environment by the reaction solution during the testing process, which is revolutionary for monitoring pathogenic bacteria.
The full experimental section can be found in the Supplementary Information.

As we all know, as a new type of nanomaterials, CDs have attracted extensive attention due to their excellent fluorescence emission and biocompatibility (Dai et al., 2022). However, the doping of alkali metals into CDs to confer multifunctionality for highly fluorescent and nanomaterial-modulated (NMM)-Fenton-like reaction-triggered the construction of multifunctional nanoplatforms with peroxidase-like catalytic activity is the first report. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the TEM image indicated that the KCl@CDs are uniformly spherical with good dispersion, which is consistent with the morphology of the CDs. To further determine the elemental composition of the material, energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis was performed, and Fig. 2B–F showed that elemental K and elemental Cl were successfully doped into the CDs. The full XPS scan of the KCl@CDs and the characteristic peak spectra of K, Cl, C, O are shown in Fig. S1. The characteristic peaks for C1s, O1s, Cl2p and K2p occur at 285.08 eV, 532.08 eV, 198.1 eV and 293 eV, respectively. By further analysis, C1s can be divided into four characteristic peaks, CO, CC, C–C, C–Cl/C–OH, which are located at 284.73 eV, 284.28 eV, 285.8 eV, 286.8 eV, respectively. O1s can be divided into two characteristic peaks at 531.58 eV and 532.98 eV, attributed to OC and O–C, indicating the presence of abundant –COOH groups in KCl@CDs to be functionalized by the aptamer. The characteristic peaks of Cl2p3/2 and Cl2p1/2 were located at 198.03 eV and 199.71 eV, respectively (Murali et al., 2021). The characteristic peaks of K2p3/2 and K2s are located at 293 eV and 377.08 eV, respectively (Qian et al., 2016). In addition, the FT-IR spectra studies revealed that the KCl@CDs are rich in functional groups. The CO stretching pattern has its highest peak at 1621 cm−1, while the relatively weak peaks at 3383 cm−1 and 1091 cm−1 belong to the characteristic absorption peaks of –OH and C–H. Notably, C–Cl appears at 665 cm−1 (Zhu et al., 2020), providing further evidence for KCl doping in the CDs (Fig. S2).

For better application, the properties of KCl@CDs were explored. As shown in Fig. S3, at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm, KCl@CDs has the maximum fluorescence emission intensity (Fig. S3). Furthermore, under the optimal excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission intensity of KCl@CDs was significantly higher than that of the PBS buffer solution (Fig. S4). In addition to fluorescence properties, we used 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as an indicator to explore the catalytic mechanism of KCl@CDs for H2O2 through electron spin resonance (ESR) energy spectrum. As shown in Fig. 2G, the intensity of the four signal peaks of EPR was 1:2:2:1, which proved that •OH was the main free radical produced in the catalytic process (Chen et al., 2020). Thus, when TMB was added to the system of KCl@CDs and H2O2, the nanozyme catalyzed the production of free •OH from H2O2, resulting in the colorless TMB changing to the blue product oxTMB (Fig. S5) (Pan et al., 2022). To explore the affinity of KCl@CDs for the substrate, the Mitchell constant (Km) was used as a parameter. A smaller Km implies a higher affinity of the enzyme to the substrate (Zhang et al., 2022). Therefore, the Km values of KCl@CDs for TMB and H2O2 were probed through the Michaelis-Menten equation. The Km for TMB as the substrate was 0.805 mM. Similarly, Km was 0.27 mM when H2O2 was used as the substrate (Figs. S6A–D). The KCl@CDs of Km were acceptable or lower compared to other nanozymes developed in previous studies (Table S1). Thus, KCl@CDs have a high affinity for H2O2 and TMB, indicating that KCl@CDs have high peroxidase activity. In addition, after 40 days of dispersion in deionized water, good stability was found for both fluorescence and peroxidase activity of KCl@CDs (Fig. S7).

To verify the successful functionalization of KCl@CDs by aptamer, the fluorescence spectra and UV absorption spectra of KCl@CDs and apt/KCl@CDs were measured, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2H, the fluorescence spectra of KCl@CDs had a 6 nm redshift relative to apt/KCl@CDs, indicating that the surface polarity of KCl@CDs was altered when coupled to the aptamer. Similarly, apt/KCl@CDs shown a peak at 255 nm compared to KCl@CDs, which was attributed to the characteristic peak of aptamer modified on the KCl@CDs surface (Fig. 2I). Furthermore, the zeta potential of apt/KCl@CDs was more negative than that of KCl@CDs (Fig. 2K). The hydration particle size measurement revealed that the hydration particle size of apt/KCl@CDs was 31.13 nm and that of KCl@CDs was 17.25 nm (Fig. 2J), which also demonstrated the successful modification of aptamer to KCl@CDs. XPS and FTIR spectroscopy are classical techniques for characterizing grafting success. As shown in Figs. S8A–G, apt/KCl@CDs contained C, N, O, K, Cl and P, which were attributed to the elements of KCl@CDs and aptamers. In addition, the N–CO structure confirms that the KCl@CDs are tightly bound to the aptamer through amide bonds. The FTIR results showed that apt/KCl@CDs have bands similar to the original aptamer at about 1200–800 cm−1 and 1500-1200 cm−1 (Fig. S8H), where the ranges indicate the vibrations of phosphate groups and nucleic acid bases (Chung et al., 2020). More rigorously, the good stability of apt/KCl@CDs was confirmed by evaluating FTIR spectra of different dates (Fig. S8I).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the optimization of conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to construct the optimal tri-modal sensing platform, we took 106 cfu/mL of S. aureus as the model and optimized the detection conditions respectively.

Temperature optimization: It is well known that temperature plays an important role in catalytic H2O2, as shown in Fig. 3 A, the highest signal value of the reaction was observed when the temperature was 25 °C. Unlike the optimal catalytic temperature of 37 °C for most natural enzymes (Zhan et al., 2020), the enzyme catalytic temperature chosen for this study was 25 °C. In addition, we found that KCl@CDs have high catalytic activity in all 5–50 °C. Therefore, the developed KCl@CDs have strong applicability.

Optimization of buffer pH: The pH of the buffer is also an important factor influencing the enzyme activity and fluorescence emission. The absorbance of the colorimetric assay was shown in Fig. 3C. The absorbance of the reaction system was highest when the pH of the HAc-NaAc buffer was 4.5. Therefore, the pH of HAc-NaAc buffer was chosen to be 4.5. Unlike the enzymatic reaction where the buffer was acidic, the fluorescence values showed optimal fluorescence emission in a slightly alkaline environment as shown in Fig. 3H. The fluorescence values tended to stabilize in the trend of the buffer being acidic to neutral, reaching the pH of 9 when the fluorescence values were strongest. The fluorescence intensity gradually tended to decrease as the pH increases, indicating that the fluorescence emission of KCl@CDs was affected by the alkaline environment.

Incubation time: In this study, we incubated M-MWCNTs-KCl@CDs with S. aureus for different times (10–80 min), respectively, and after magnetic separation, measured the signal intensity in fluorescence modal and colorimetric modal. As shown in Fig. 3B and F, both the colorimetric and fluorescence assays showed an increasing trend in signal values as the incubation time increased until 60 min when the signal stabilized. As expected, the nature of the intensity of the colorimetric and fluorescence values was the amounts of KCl@CDs displaced by the bacteria on M-MWCNTs-apt/KCl@CDs, and when the incubation time reached 60 min, the KCl@CDs under displacement reached saturation, the absorbance and fluorescence values of this assay platform reached the strongest.

Optimization of apt/KCl@CDs concentration: In order to obtain the best detection sensitivity for the platform, it is necessary to optimize the concentration of apt/KCl@CDs. As in Fig. 3D, G and 3K, the absorbance, fluorescence values and temperature signal of the detection platform increased with increasing concentration of apt/KCl@CDs until the signal values stabilized after the concentration of 4 mg/mL, thus, we chose 4 mg/mL of apt/KCl@CDs as the optimum concentration.

Optimization of irradiation time: In order to achieve a stable temperature signal, we optimized the illumination time for the blue product, as shown in Fig. 3J, when the illumination time was 200 s, the signal reached stability, so we chose the illumination time of 200 s.

Finally, the feasibility of the integrated platform was tested under optimal conditions, and different conditions were prepared to explore the signal efficiency of the aptasensor. As shown in Fig. 3E, I and 3L, the signal value of the supernatant after magnetic separation in group (I) far exceeds that of the other components, indicating that the aptasensor has a high sensitivity to S. aureus. Group (II) showed the signal of this integrated platform when 0 cfu/mL of S. aureus was present, from which it was concluded that although there was some background signal, the interference was weak. Group (III) showed that we were very successful in washing M-MWCNTs, causing almost negligible background interference. Group (IV) was the control group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Staphylococcus aureus in pure culture by aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: With addition of S. aureus in the reaction system, apt/KCl@CDs adsorbed on M-MWCNTs were displaced by the target bacteria, and the S. aureus-apt/KCl@CDs in the supernatant drove the detection signals of the tri-modals (Fig. 4 A). The results showed that the signal intensity of the fluorescence detection modal increases with the increase of S. aureus concentration (Fig. 4B), which was attributed to the fact that the higher the concentration of the target bacteria, the more apt/KCl@CDs were displaced on the M-MWCNTs, and output stronger fluorescence intensity. It was found that the fluorescence intensity showed a linear relationship with the concentration of the target bacteria at concentrations of 101-107 cfu/mL (Fig. 4C). The linear relationship equation was Normalized FL Intensity = 0.1328 Lg [S. aureus (cfu/mL)] + 0.0794, the linear correlation coefficient R2 = 0.994 (Fig. 4D). The detection limit (LOD) for the fluorescence assay was 4.81 cfu/mL and the results were also calculated from 3σ/slope, where σ represents the standard deviation of the fluorescence values from 11 measurements of the blank sample (Shen et al., 2023). Similarly, when H2O2 and TMB were added to the supernatant, the extent to which TMB was catalyzed was proportional to the concentration of the target bacteria, and the color gradually changed from colorless to dark blue (Fig. 4H). The absorbance of the blue product at 654 nm gradually increases (Fig. 4E). It was found an excellent linearity of the absorbance values with the concentration of the target bacteria at 101-107 cfu/mL (Fig. 4F). As shown in Fig. 4G, the linear equation of absorbance versus bacterial concentration was absorbance = 0.1442 Lg [S. aureus (cfu/mL)] + 0.0757, with a linear correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9968. In addition, the LOD for the colorimetric assay was 3.40 cfu/mL. Since the blue product has an absorption peak at 654 nm, the temperature change generated by the 660 nm laser irradiation for 200 s is positively correlated with the intensity of the absorption peak. (Fig. 4H). Photothermal modal detection can further ensure the correctness of the test results and improve the accuracy. It was observed that the change in temperature signal was positively correlated with bacterial concentration, when the bacteria concentration is 101-107 cfu/mL, the quantitative detection could be achieved (Fig. 4I), and the detection equation was temperature = 4.2776 Lg [S. aureus (cfu/mL)] + 28.6296, R2 = 0.9822 (Fig. 4J). The LOD of the photothermal detection platform was 6.74 cfu/mL. The sensitivity of our developed tir-modal sensing platform was comparable or higher compared to other reported S. aureus detection platforms (Table S2). Notably, the aptasensor has good stability (Fig. S14), and all of the above confirms that our developed tri-modal sensing platform has a very high potential for application in the control of pathogenic bacteria.

The environment in the samples is very complex and therefore selectivity is important for the assay. S. aureus, E. coli, S. typhimurium, L. monocytogenes, P. aeruginosa, V. parahaemolyticus, C. sakazakii and a sterile PBS blank control were tested under the same conditions using our developed tri-mode aptasensor. Fig. 4K-M and Figs. S15A–B showed that, except for the target bacteria S. aureus, there was no significant difference between the other pathogenic bacteria and the control group. As a result, we have developed a tri-modal aptasensor that was specific to S. aureus and could selectively identify S. aureus in environments that contain multiple pathogenic bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practicality of tri-modal detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the detection capability of the proposed tri-modally driven aptasensor for real samples, we manually spiked three concentrations of S. aureus in each of the samples (wastewater treated by the factory, milk, and beef) and used the developed tri-modal aptasensor for quantitative detection. As shown in Table 1 , the tri-modal identification platform had excellent recoveries of 85–111%, which was sufficient to demonstrate the accuracy of the strategy. In addition, the assay modal had a standard deviation of less than 12% for S. aureus, sufficient to demonstrate its superior quantitative capability. Finally, we used the standard plate counting method to compare with the detection results of this study, and the results showed that there was no obvious difference between the two detection methods (Fig. S16). Notably, compared to the traditional culture method (5–7 days), the aptasensor only takes about 80 min, which is easy to operate with tri-modal of signals. Therefore, the developed aptasensor has the advantages of stability, sensitivity and accuracy, and has great potential for practical detection applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integrated platform for the elimination of Staphylococcus aureus
ce:section.ce:para: This study also evaluated the sterilization efficiency and sterilization mechanism of the integrated platform. As shown in Fig. 5 A, it could be seen in plate counting and fluorescence microscopy that the strategy was able to achieve efficient bactericidal activity only when apt/KCl@CDs were present together with H2O2 and was significantly different compared to the other fractions (Fig. 5B). In the SEM, it could be seen that except for slight damage to the bacteria in group H2O2 and group apt/KCl@CDs, the other components are only negligibly different from the control, which was due to the slight bactericidal nature of the low concentration of H2O2. In addition, since the bacteria have trace amounts of H2O2, the group apt/KCl@CDs are also able to produce a small amount of •OH, whereas non-KCl doped CDs do not oxidize TMB to turn blue and did not possess peroxidase activity (Fig. 5E), we speculated that this is due to KCl@CDs during the hydrothermal synthesis process, KCl converts the oxygen-containing functional groups in lignin into carboxyl and carbon oxygen double bonds (Sun et al., 2015), increasing the binding site of H2O2 and promoting the production of •OH. As seen above, separate KCl@CDs and low concentrations of H2O2 will only cause subtle damage to the bacteria, and the main sterilization method of this aptasensor is the toxic •OH produced by KCl@CDs catalyzing H2O2.

Fig. 5C and D further indicated the breakage of the cells in the experimental group. As a cytoplasmic enzyme, lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) leaks into the solution when cell membrane rupture occurs, and Fig. 5C showed that the content of LDH increases with increasing sterilization time. PO4–P is an important component of the cell membrane skeleton, which is released from the cell membrane when it ruptures. Fig. 5D showed the measurement results of PO4–P content, and it can be seen that the content of PO4–P increased with the treatment. Therefore, leakage of both LDH and PO4–P indicated a disruption of the structural integrity of the cell.

When the detection procedure was completed, apt/KCl@CDs in the supernatant were adsorbed on the bacterial surface, and we hypothesized that the adsorption of aptamers would help the reactive oxygen species to better destroy the bacterial structure in the presence of low concentrations of nanozymes, as shown in Fig. 5F, the bactericidal effect of apt/KCl@CDs was significantly better than that of KCl@CDs at the same concentration, this is due to KCl@CDs after modifying the aptamer, it could specifically bind to the target bacteria, avoiding its dispersion and aggregation to a specific point in the liquid environment, allowing the •OH to accumulate on various parts of the bacterial surface, thus improving the bactericidal efficiency (Fig. 5H). In addition, after confirming the applicability of the platform for testing in real samples, the sterilization efficiency in real samples was also evaluated in this study. As shown in Fig. 5G, when 106 cfu/mL of S. aureus was added to the actual sample, there was a significant difference between the treatment group compared to the control group (directly spiked samples), thus showing the positive significance of this integrated platform for the control of actual samples.

In summary, we have synthesized KCl@CDs and magnetic M-MWCNTs, respectively. By the specific recognition ability of the aptamer and its own nature as single-stranded DNA, apt/KCl@CDs were complexed with M-MWCNTs to form a detection platform. Compared to conventional CDs, which only have a fluorescent signal, in this study, the peroxidase activity of KCl@CDs was used to catalyze the change of TMB to a blue product (oxTMB) for colorimetric detection. Cleverly, the absorption peak of the oxTMB at 654 nm can also be used for quantitative detection in photothermal modal. Unlike other methods that use MWCNTs as a quenching agent, this study has utilized MWCNTs to chemically couple with CIP to confer magnetism to the carrier, which effectively avoids the interference of the quenching agent on the signal output (Pourreza et al., 2022). In addition, the signal values of the supernatant were determined by the inverse strategy, avoiding the interference of magnetic nanomaterials on the signal when other strategies utilize the sandwich structure principle for detection. In short, novel carbon nanocomposites with built-in cross-reference correction were developed for the tri-modal colorimetric-fluorescence-photothermal detection of S. aureus with high selectivity and sensitivity. The detection limits of the fluorescence, colorimetric, and photothermal detection platforms were 4.81, 3.40, and 6.74 cfu/mL, respectively. And they all had good quantitative capabilities (101-107 cfu/mL). In addition, when the test result is positive, due to the adsorption of aptamer on the target bacteria, the KCl@CDs gather on the surface of the bacteria and implement enhanced sterilization. The sterilization rate is nearly 100%. It is noteworthy that the “five birds with one stone” platform developed in this study amalgamates magnetic separation, tri-modal precision detection, and efficient sterilization. Through the intelligent combination of ultra-precise detection and on-demand sterilization, it avoids secondary contamination during the detection process. At the same time, it provides a novel idea for immediate control of pathogenic bacteria contamination as well as ensuring environmental and food safety. Ultimately, to successfully introduce the developed aptasensor into the clinical market, several challenges must be addressed. The detection system should be expanded and optimized for more complex clinical samples. Furthermore, the aptasensor can be combined with intelligent testing devices to create relevant commercial kits and identification databases, thus standardizing the testing process and improving testing efficiency.

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development program, China (2022YFF1100702; 2022YFF1100703); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32230084; 32102104); the Guangdong Provincial Key Research and Development Project (2022B1111040002); and the Anhui Natural Science Foundation (2208085J11).

Yuwei Ren: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Lulu Cao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation. Rui Jiao: Writing – review & editing. Xiyan Zhang: Data curation. Hong yuan Zhao: Formal analysis. Zhao Liang: Methodology. Guoqing Li: Software. Na Ling: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yingwang Ye: Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.
